questionText: Express $8\sin\theta + 6\cos\theta$ in the form $R\sin(\theta+\alpha)$, where $R>0$ and $0^\circ < \alpha < 90^\circ$. Hence write down the maximum value of $8\sin\theta + 6\cos\theta$.

steps.description: Set up the identity to match
steps.explanation: We want the two-term expression written as a single sine wave. Setting it identically equal to $R\sin(\theta+\alpha)$ lets us expand the right-hand side and match coefficients.

steps.description: Expand the target with the addition formula
steps.explanation: Using $\sin(\theta+\alpha)=\sin\theta\cos\alpha+\cos\theta\sin\alpha$ puts the right-hand side in the same form as the left: a multiple of $\sin\theta$ plus a multiple of $\cos\theta$.

steps.description: Compare coefficients of sinθ and cosθ
steps.explanation: Matching the amount of $\sin\theta$ on each side gives $R\cos\alpha=8$, and matching the amount of $\cos\theta$ gives $R\sin\alpha=6$.

steps.description: Find R by squaring and adding
steps.explanation: Squaring and adding the two equations eliminates $\alpha$ through $\cos^2\alpha+\sin^2\alpha=1$, leaving $R^2=8^2+6^2=100$. The positive root gives $R=10$.

steps.description: Divide the equations to isolate tanα
steps.explanation: Dividing the two equations cancels $R$ and leaves $\tan\alpha=\dfrac{6}{8}$, which simplifies to $\dfrac{3}{4}$.

steps.description: Solve for the acute angle α
steps.explanation: Both $8$ and $6$ are positive, so $\alpha$ is acute. The inverse tangent gives $\alpha=36.9^\circ$ to $1$ decimal place.
Putting the amplitude and phase together gives the single sine wave equivalent to the original expression.
Since $\sin$ of any angle never exceeds $1$, the expression $10\sin(\theta+36.9^\circ)$ is greatest when the sine equals $1$. Its maximum value is therefore simply $R=10$, with no calculus needed.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $8\sin\theta + 6\cos\theta = 10\sin(\theta + 36.9^\circ)$, so the maximum value of the expression is $10$.
